_meta.scenario: Senior engineer Akarsh, Monday 2026-05-25 09:00–10:00 IST. Primary focus KAN-139 (this very task — golden dataset). Brief KAN-136 (observability) check. Interruption mid-hour for KAN-138 (mlx merge sidequest). Realistic non-task interruptions: Slack triage, Twitter peek, Stack Overflow tangent, YouTube background.

workflow_pattern.default_coding: Claude Code running inside VS Code's integrated terminal (app_name='Code'). User authors prompts; Claude Code reads context, writes/edits files, shows diffs; user approves, asks follow-up questions, or requests changes. The user is reviewer/director, not typist.
exceptions: Small inline edits in VS Code itself — rename a variable, fix a typo, accept an autocomplete suggestion. app_name='Code', no terminal in focus.
Direct shell commands (git, cargo, npm, sqlite3) executed in VS Code's integrated terminal or in standalone Terminal.app. app_name='Code' if inside VS Code, else 'Terminal'.
Mixed sessions where the user runs a quick shell command while Claude Code is between turns — captured in the same Code/Terminal session.
workflow_pattern.ui_elements_to_include: Claude Code conversation UI (⏺ preamble lines, ● tool calls with file paths + result summaries, diff boxes with approval prompts), VS Code chrome (Explorer tree with new files appearing, Source Control modified-files list, status bar with branch name + unsaved indicators), bottom-panel tab strip (Problems, Output, Debug Console, TERMINAL, Ports), file-tabs at top.
workflow_pattern.non_coding_apps: Chrome / Slack / Claude desktop / YouTube / DBeaver / Terminal.app — keep app_name accurate. Sessions in those apps follow their own UI patterns, not Claude Code.

ground_truth.reasoning: Morning Slack triage. User opens Slack, reads unread messages in #eng-meridian (mentions of KAN-136 observability work and KAN-138 merge done by a teammate, plus a figma mockup nudge), opens a Friday thread to re-read its replies, then checks a DM from Aditya about KAN-139 ownership, then peeks at #general for a team-lunch reminder, then leaves. No editor open, no commits, no decisions made — purely reading inbox. Mentions of multiple ticket keys (KAN-136, KAN-138, KAN-139) are about other people's work or future work, not the user's current activity.

sessions.design_notes: Hard trap for the classifier. session_text contains KAN-136, KAN-138, and KAN-139 verbatim — three different ticket keys, any of which a keyword-matcher would latch onto. Correct answer is overhead because the activity is reading, not working. Tests the model's ability to distinguish 'ticket is mentioned in screen text' from 'user is doing work on that ticket'. This is one of the most common real-world failure modes: morning Slack catch-ups would otherwise be mis-attributed to whichever ticket got mentioned most.

ground_truth.reasoning: User created a new branch named 'feat/golden-dataset-kan-139' — the branch name literally encodes the ticket key. Then listed services/tests/evals/ (the eval harness directory referenced in KAN-139's description). The git log shows the recent observability work from Friday (KAN-136) but the user has now branched away from that to start KAN-139. Strong unambiguous signal despite only 15s duration.

sessions.design_notes: Tests two principles at once: (1) duration is not a proxy for signal — 15s is plenty when the user just runs 'git checkout -b feat/golden-dataset-kan-139'; (2) the classifier should prefer branch-name evidence over git-log evidence. The git log mentions 5 KAN-136 commits and zero KAN-139 commits — a naive token-frequency classifier would label this KAN-136. The correct signal is the current branch name, not the commit history. Also seeds the prior-session context for sessions 3+ — once this is classified KAN-139, subsequent Code sessions in services/tests/evals/ inherit a strong KAN-139 prior.

KAN-139
Create golden dataset for task classification
Attach   Add a child issue   Link issue   Apps
Description
Write more than 50 user flows with the help of Claude that represents a developer's coding cycle and the correct task (on Jira) classification for their activities during these flows. The dataset feeds the deepeval-based eval harness in services/tests/evals/ and is the foundation for benchmarking model swaps and prompt iterations.
Acceptance criteria
• At least 50 scoreable golden sessions across realistic developer flows
• Mix of easy / medium / hard / overhead / untracked difficulty tiers
• Drop-in to existing build_dataset.py format (input prompt + expected_output JSON)
• Documented difficulty taxonomy and design rationale per session
• Both real-project (Meridian KAN-xxx) and generic-placeholder (PROJ-xxx) developer personas

"""Build tests/evals/.dataset.json from real labeled sessions in meridian.db.

Two modes:

  SESSION_IDS mode (preferred for curated eval sets):
    Export specific sessions by ID. Bypasses routing/confidence filters.
    SESSION_IDS=2276,2354,1961,2181,1792,1972,2514 \\
      MERIDIAN_DB=~/.meridian/meridian.db \\
      python tests/evals/build_dataset.py

  Bulk mode (general export):
    Queries sessions where task_method = 'hermes_aiagent' and routing is
    'auto' or 'pending' (hermes sessions use 'pending') with high confidence.
    MERIDIAN_DB=~/.meridian/meridian.db python tests/evals/build_dataset.py

Options (env vars):
    MERIDIAN_DB     Path to meridian.db  (default: ~/.meridian/meridian.db)
    SESSION_IDS     Comma-separated session IDs to export (overrides bulk query)
    MIN_CONFIDENCE  Minimum confidence for bulk mode (default: 0.85)
    LIMIT           Max sessions for bulk mode (default: 100)

The output overwrites tests/evals/.dataset.json.
Review and spot-check the exported goldens before committing them.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import os
import sqlite3
import sys
from pathlib import Path

_SERVICES_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent.parent
if str(_SERVICES_DIR) not in sys.path:
    sys.path.insert(0, str(_SERVICES_DIR))

from agents._prompts import build_user_message

MERIDIAN_DB = Path(os.environ.get("MERIDIAN_DB", Path.home() / ".meridian/meridian.db"))
SESSION_IDS: list[int] = [
    int(x.strip())
    for x in os.environ.get("SESSION_IDS", "").split(",")
    if x.strip().isdigit()
]
MIN_CONFIDENCE = float(os.environ.get("MIN_CONFIDENCE", "0.85"))
LIMIT = int(os.environ.get("LIMIT", "100"))
OUTPUT = Path(__file__).parent / ".dataset.json"

_NULL_TASK_KEYS = {"none", "null", "n/a", "nil", "undefined", "hermes_aiagent", ""}

def _normalise_task_key(raw: str | None) -> str:
    if raw is None:
        return "none"
    if raw.strip().lower() in _NULL_TASK_KEYS:
        return "none"
    return raw.strip()


_SESSION_COLS = (
    "id, app_name, started_at, ended_at, duration_s, session_text,"
    " session_text_source, window_titles, category, confidence,"
    " task_key, task_routing, task_method, task_session_type,"
    " COALESCE(task_reasoning, '') AS task_reasoning"
)


def _fetch_sessions_by_ids(con: sqlite3.Connection, ids: list[int]) -> list[dict]:
    placeholders = ",".join("?" * len(ids))
    rows = con.execute(
        f"SELECT {_SESSION_COLS}"
        f" FROM app_sessions"
        f" WHERE id IN ({placeholders})"
        f" ORDER BY id",
        ids,
    ).fetchall()
    return [dict(r) for r in rows]


def _fetch_labeled_sessions(con: sqlite3.Connection) -> list[dict]:
    rows = con.execute(
        f"SELECT {_SESSION_COLS}"
        " FROM app_sessions"
        " WHERE task_method IN ('hermes_aiagent', 'mlx_direct')"
        "   AND task_routing IN ('auto', 'pending')"
        "   AND task_confidence >= ?"
        " ORDER BY id DESC"
        " LIMIT ?",
        (MIN_CONFIDENCE, LIMIT),
    ).fetchall()
    return [dict(r) for r in rows]

def _fetch_recent(con: sqlite3.Connection, before_id: int) -> list[dict]:
    rows = con.execute(
        "SELECT app_name, started_at, duration_s, task_key, task_routing, category"
        " FROM app_sessions"
        " WHERE id < ? AND duration_s > 1 AND COALESCE(session_text,'') != ''"
        " ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 5",
        (before_id,),
    ).fetchall()
    result = [dict(r) for r in rows]
    result.reverse()
    return result

"""Metrics for the Stage 3 session→task classifier eval suite.

Import metric lists from here into eval files. Do not construct metrics inline
in eval files — keep them here so thresholds stay in one place.

expected_output format (JSON string):
    {"task_key": "KAN-107" | "none", "session_type": "task"|"overhead"|"untracked",
     "reasoning": "<ground truth reasoning from original classifier>"}

actual_output format (JSON string from classifier):
    Same shape — task_key, session_type, reasoning.
    Callers may also pass a plain task_key string for backward compat.

Two evaluation levels:
  AGENT_E2E_METRICS   — end-to-end trace, attached to @observe on outer agent fn
  CLASSIFIER_METRICS  — component span, exact-match on task_key + session_type
"""

class TaskKeyMatchMetric(BaseMetric):
    """Exact-match on task_key — no LLM call required.

    Handles both JSON expected_output and plain task_key strings.
    None / 'none' / 'null' / 'n/a' / '' treated as equivalent null labels.
    """

    def __init__(self, threshold: float = 1.0):
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.score: float = 0.0
        self.success: bool = False
        self.reason: str = ""
        self.error: str | None = None

    @property
    def __name__(self) -> str:
        return "TaskKeyMatch"

    def measure(self, test_case: LLMTestCase) -> float:
        try:
            predicted = _normalise_key(_parse_actual(test_case.actual_output).get("task_key"))
            expected  = _normalise_key(_parse_expected(test_case.expected_output).get("task_key"))
            self.score = 1.0 if predicted == expected else 0.0
            self.reason = f"predicted={predicted!r} expected={expected!r}"
            self.success = self.score >= self.threshold
        except Exception as exc:
            self.error = str(exc)
            self.score = 0.0
            self.success = False
            raise
        return self.score

from __future__ import annotations
import json
from pathlib import Path
import pytest
from deepeval.dataset import EvaluationDataset, Golden
from deepeval import evaluate
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase
from agents.run_task_linker_mlx import classify_session_blocking
from tests.evals.metrics import CLASSIFIER_METRICS

DATASET = Path(__file__).parent / ".dataset.json"

@pytest.mark.eval
def test_mlx_classifier_against_dataset():
    payload = json.loads(DATASET.read_text())
    goldens = [Golden(**g) for g in payload]
    dataset = EvaluationDataset(goldens=goldens)

    cases: list[LLMTestCase] = []
    for golden in dataset.goldens:
        actual = classify_session_blocking(golden.input)
        cases.append(LLMTestCase(
            input=golden.input,
            actual_output=actual,
            expected_output=golden.expected_output,
        ))

    results = evaluate(test_cases=cases, metrics=CLASSIFIER_METRICS)
    failed = [r for r in results.test_results if not r.success]
    print(f"Passed {len(results.test_results) - len(failed)} / {len(results.test_results)}")

def _format_recent_sessions(sessions: list[dict]) -> str:
    if not sessions:
        return "  (no recent session context)"
    rows = []
    for s in sessions:
        time_str = _fmt_time(s.get("started_at") or "")
        app = (s.get("app_name") or "?")[:14]
        dur_str = _fmt_dur(s.get("duration_s") or 0)
        task_key = s.get("task_key")
        routing = s.get("task_routing")
        category = (s.get("category") or "").strip()
        if task_key:
            target = f"→ {task_key}"
        elif routing == "untracked":
            target = "→ [untracked]"
        elif routing is None:
            target = "→ [pending]"
        else:
            target = "→ [overhead]"
        cat_tag = f"  [{category}]" if category else ""
        rows.append(f"  {time_str}  {app:<14}  {dur_str:<7}  {target}{cat_tag}")
    return "\n".join(rows)

Claude Sonnet 4.6 — 09:06 AM
Good framing. Before generating, the dataset's value lives in *what it discriminates*. A few thoughts:

**Difficulty taxonomy — concrete failure modes to target:**

1. **Keyword-mention overhead** — the trap where session_text contains a ticket key but the user isn't working on it. Slack messages, GitHub backlog views, Jira comment threads. This is your single highest-volume real-world false positive. Make sure 3–5 sessions exploit this directly.

2. **Same-app context switch** — user in Chrome on a KAN-XXX tab, then opens YouTube/Twitter in same Chrome process. Classifier shouldn't carry the prior just because app_name is identical.

3. **Short high-signal sessions** — `git checkout -b feat/...` in 15s tells you the next 20 minutes are about that ticket. Your current build_dataset.py filters duration_s > 1; this is partly a context-window architecture problem.

4. **Ambiguous between two real tickets** — editing _prompts.py could be KAN-139 (eval prompts) or KAN-136 (observability prompts). The model needs the *surrounding* context (branch name, recent sessions, what was just edited) to disambiguate.

5. **Work-but-no-ticket (untracked)** — fixing a flaky CI test, resolving an unrelated merge conflict, writing internal docs. Genuinely productive but no candidate matches. The classifier must not force a label here.

6. **Decoy tickets** — add 1–2 synthetic candidates that are semantically adjacent to real work (e.g. a fake 'Worklog write-back' ticket while user works on 'eval dataset'). Tests whether the model picks the *closer* match or gets distracted.

**On the recent-session context window:**
Your code grabs the last 5 sessions by id with duration_s > 1. With your 7-second median session length, 5 sessions ≈ 35 seconds of history — barely a context-switch's worth. Worth A/B-ing against a time-window strategy (last N minutes of meaningful sessions). The golden dataset should encode full timestamps so both rendering strategies can be evaluated on the same examples.

Want me to draft the difficulty distribution per persona?

That's the right architecture — separate the data (structured sessions + ground truth) from the prompt rendering. The builder becomes the contract surface.

**Per-persona distribution (35 sessions, ~20–25 scoreable):**

| Difficulty | Count | What it tests |
|---|---|---|
| Easy (clear app + clear ticket) | 5 | Baseline accuracy |
| Medium (needs prior-session context) | 4 | Does the recent-context block earn its weight? |
| Hard (ambiguous between 2 real tickets) | 3 | Discrimination quality |
| Hard-decoy (real vs synthetic-decoy) | 2 | Resistance to plausible-wrong candidates |
| Overhead — comms (Slack, email) | 2 | False-positive defense vs work-mentioning chat |
| Overhead — entertainment (YouTube, Twitter) | 2 | False-positive defense vs idle browsing |
| Untracked work | 2 | Don't force a label when no candidate fits |
| Context-only (sub-scoreable transitions) | 10–15 | Realistic timeline density |

The context-only sessions don't get scored individually but they populate the recent-context block for the scored ones, so they need to be coherent with the surrounding narrative.

**Suggested seed schema per session:**
```json
{
  "id": 1,
  "app_name": "Code",
  "started_at": "2026-05-25T09:00:00+05:30",
  "ended_at":   "2026-05-25T09:02:34+05:30",
  "duration_s": 154,
  "window_titles": [{"window_name": "...", "count": N}],
  "session_text": "<realistic OCR+a11y text>",
  "ground_truth": {"task_key": "...", "session_type": "...", "reasoning": "...", "difficulty": "..."},
  "design_notes": "<what failure mode this case targets>"
}
```

The `design_notes` field is the most underrated part — when the dataset is used for self-improvement (analyze failures → tune prompt → re-eval), being able to grep for which failure-mode tests are failing is the difference between 'eval pass rate went up' and 'we fixed the keyword-mention false-positive class'. -- Duration distribution to understand 'how long is a meaningful session'
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN duration_s < 30  THEN '0-30s'
    WHEN duration_s < 60  THEN '30-60s'
    WHEN duration_s < 120 THEN '1-2m'
    WHEN duration_s < 300 THEN '2-5m'
    WHEN duration_s < 600 THEN '5-10m'
    WHEN duration_s < 1800 THEN '10-30m'
    ELSE '30m+'
  END bucket,
  COUNT(*) cnt,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN task_key IS NOT NULL THEN 1 END) classified
FROM app_sessions GROUP BY bucket ORDER BY MIN(duration_s);

ground_truth.reasoning: User opens DBeaver against meridian.db and runs four ad-hoc SQL queries to inform synthetic dataset generation: (1) app/session-type distribution to confirm which apps and modes the synthetic data should cover, (2) duration distribution to understand 'how long is meaningful', (3) sample a real KAN-107 classified session to see real session_text shape, (4) open candidate ticket list from pm_tasks. Every query is squarely about the data-shape research for the golden dataset deliverable — directly relevant to KAN-139.

sessions.design_notes: Tests classification on DBeaver (third distinct app type this hour). Failure modes: (1) the query at 09:08:51 returns a list of ticket keys (KAN-107, KAN-109, KAN-67, etc.) — a classifier looking at high-frequency ticket keys would match KAN-107 (top of list, 142 sessions); wrong, that's historical data the user is inspecting, not their current task. (2) the final query reads pm_tasks and shows KAN-139 alongside KAN-136, KAN-138, etc. — the classifier must pick KAN-139 because that's the user's task even though all open tickets are visible. (3) DBeaver activity often gets misclassified as 'deployment_devops' in your real data (the category field defaults to that) — the eval should test that task_key inference works independent of the category-stage misclassification. (4) v2 adds the persistent Database Navigator tree + query History + Output panel + status bar chrome periodically across frames, matching real DBeaver a11y density (~138 chars/sec).

def _fetch_recent(con: sqlite3.Connection, before_id: int) -> list[dict]:
    rows = con.execute(
        "SELECT app_name, started_at, duration_s, task_key, task_routing, category"
        " FROM app_sessions"
        " WHERE id < ? AND duration_s > 1 AND COALESCE(session_text,'') != ''"
        " ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 5",
        (before_id,),
    ).fetchall()
    result = [dict(r) for r in rows]
    result.reverse()
    return result

"""Render hand-authored seed sessions into the deepeval Golden format.

Bridge between golden_seed/dev_<persona>_sessions.json (structured + ground truth)
and .dataset.json (the deepeval input/expected_output shape build_dataset.py emits).

claude — meridian — TERMINAL
⏺ Uses started_at for both ends — `t0 = target.started_at - 10m`, then filters
  `prior.started_at >= t0`. So a 15s session that started 7 minutes before the target's
  started_at gets included regardless of when it ended. ended_at would risk pulling in
  sessions that overlapped with the target (technically future-in-progress), which we
  don't want in a causal classifier eval.

  Concrete example:
    target.started_at = 2026-05-25T09:15:00 (later session)
    t0                = 09:05:00
    prior #1: started_at=09:07:30, duration_s=15  (your short git checkout)
      → 09:07:30 >= 09:05:00  → included ✓
    prior #2: started_at=09:14:50, duration_s=30, ended_at=09:15:20 (overlaps target)
      → 09:14:50 >= 09:05:00  → would be included, but this is causally invalid
        because the prior session was still in-progress when the target started.
        For now this is a known edge case — your seeds are non-overlapping so it
        doesn't bite. Worth a TODO if you start generating overlapping prior data.

  Worth flagging: 'scoreable but short' anchors are exactly the kind of context that's
  cheap to lose. Worth a follow-up — should we sort the recent block by started_at desc
  inside _to_recent? Current order is preserved from the seeds (chronological), matching
  what build_dataset.py does, but the classifier prompt format implies most-recent-first.

> walk me through the decoy adjacency reasoning. for PROJ-225 (PostHog migration) and
  PROJ-230 (admin dashboard), what session content would tempt a classifier into picking
  the decoy instead of the right ticket? i want to know what specific Dev B sessions
  later will exploit those decoys.

✻ Thinking…
[09:16:04]
claude — meridian — TERMINAL
⏺ Good question — the decoys earn their keep when a Dev B session has content that
  sits *between* a real ticket and the decoy. Two scenarios where each decoy bites:

  **PROJ-225 (PostHog migration) as a decoy:**

  The danger ticket is PROJ-210 (onboarding email A/B test). Both touch analytics
  pipelines, both deal with events tables, both involve experiment-style variant
  assignment. A Dev B session where the developer is:

    • editing a file like experiments/onboarding_assignment.py — looks like PROJ-210
    • OR adding event-emit calls (track('signup', {...})) — looks like PROJ-225
    • OR running a SQL migration adding columns to events_received — could be either

  The discriminator should be: is the file/branch named for the experiment harness
  (PROJ-210) or the analytics vendor (PROJ-225)? If the session text shows 'mixpanel'
  or 'posthog' import lines, that's PROJ-225. If it shows 'experiments.variant_for(...)'
  or '/api/experiments/assign', that's PROJ-210. A classifier that just sees 'analytics
  events' will guess wrong about half the time, which is exactly what we want to test.

  **PROJ-230 (admin dashboard) as a decoy:**

  The danger ticket is anything UI-adjacent — and in this dataset that's nothing real,
  which makes PROJ-230 a different kind of decoy. PROJ-230 is the trap for *untracked*
  UI work. Imagine a Dev B session where the developer is:

    • polishing a generic React component (Button.tsx, Modal.tsx)
    • debugging a tailwind class collision
    • adding a loading state to an unrelated page

  None of those map to PROJ-201/204/210/215/220 — the right answer is task_key=none,
  session_type=untracked. But the classifier sees 'React + admin-looking UI' and reaches
  for PROJ-230 because it's the closest semantic neighbor. Resisting that pull is the
  test. Specifically: PROJ-230 should ONLY be picked if the session shows clear
  support-tool-specific signals (UserLookupForm, /admin route, support-engineer
  vocabulary, manual-credit-issuance flow). Anything less specific → untracked.

  **Sessions that should exploit these decoys in dev_b_generic_sessions.json:**

  When you generate Dev B, plan at minimum:
    1. A session editing the experiments harness with PROJ-210 as the right answer —
       must include 'variant_for' / 'experiments.assign()' but NOT mention mixpanel/
       posthog. Tests that the classifier doesn't drift to PROJ-225.
    2. A session adding tracking calls during PROJ-210 work where the call happens to
       use the Mixpanel API — should still be PROJ-210 because the broader work is
       the email A/B test; the tracking call is incidental. Hard medium-to-hard case.
    3. A generic React-cleanup session that should classify as untracked, not PROJ-230.
       Specifically NO admin-route content. Tests untracked-vs-decoy boundary.
    4. A session where the developer ACTUALLY works on PROJ-230 (e.g., scaffolds the
       UserLookupForm) — clean positive that proves PROJ-230 is reachable, so the
       eval isn't just 'decoy is always wrong'. That symmetry matters for the
       classifier's calibration — if decoys are never correct, the model learns to
       ignore them and the test loses signal.

  Same logic carries to the KAN-142 / KAN-145 decoys on the Dev A side: KAN-142
  (Worklog write-back) is the trap for any session involving Jira API calls or
  worklog vocabulary; KAN-145 (Dashboard UI) is the trap for any session-card or
  badge-rendering session in the meridian UI codebase. Sessions 28–35 in your Dev A
  plan should include at least one exploit per decoy.

  **One more nuance — what the classifier should explicitly NOT do:**

  Don't treat 'decoy mentioned in candidates list' as a negative signal during
  scoring. All decoys appear in the same CANDIDATE TICKETS block the model sees,
  formatted identically to real tickets (same fields, same description shape).
  The is_decoy flag lives in the seed file, not in the prompt — by design. The
  classifier should rank candidates on semantic match strength alone; the eval
  then measures whether decoys got picked when they shouldn't have. Two
  failure-mode metrics worth logging when the eval runs:

    decoy_picked_when_correct_was_real      e.g. session was PROJ-210 (email A/B)
                                            and classifier returned PROJ-225 (PostHog)

    decoy_picked_when_correct_was_untracked e.g. session was generic React cleanup
                                            and classifier returned PROJ-230 (admin UI)

  The second is more dangerous — it's the classifier hallucinating a ticket to
  appear productive. That's exactly what KAN-139's 'Untracked work' difficulty
  tier is designed to catch. If decoy_picked_when_correct_was_untracked > 0 on
  a new model, that's a regression worth chasing even if overall accuracy is up.

  **Concrete signal patterns to bake into Dev B sessions:**

  For PROJ-210 → PROJ-225 confusion test:
    window_title:  "onboarding_assignment.py — fastify-saas"
    session_text:  "from experiments.harness import variant_for"
                   "track('signup_v2_assigned', { variant, user_id })"
    → correct: PROJ-210 (experiments harness work; the tracking call is incidental)

  For PROJ-225 clean positive (proves decoy is reachable):
    window_title:  "tracking/posthog_client.py — fastify-saas"
    session_text:  "import posthog"
                   "posthog.capture(distinct_id, event, properties)"
                   "remove mixpanel import"
    → correct: PROJ-225 (THIS is the migration work — proves decoy can win when right)

  For PROJ-230 untracked-boundary test:
    window_title:  "Button.tsx — components — fastify-saas"
    session_text:  "<Button variant='ghost' loading={isPending}>"
                   "fix tailwind class collision: hover:bg-slate-100"
    → correct: none / untracked (generic UI work, NOT the admin dashboard)

  Same skeleton applies to Dev A's KAN-142 (Worklog) and KAN-145 (Dashboard UI):
  build at least one clean-positive case per decoy plus one boundary case where
  the classifier must resist them. That symmetry is what makes the eval honest —
  if decoys are never the correct answer, the model learns to ignore them and
  the discrimination test loses its signal.

Projects / Meridian / KAN-139
KAN-139
Create golden dataset for task classification
Description
Write more than 50 user flows with the help of Claude that represents a developer's coding cycle and the correct task (on Jira) classification for their activities during these flows. The dataset feeds the deepeval-based eval harness in services/tests/evals/ and is the foundation for benchmarking model swaps and prompt iterations.
Acceptance criteria
• At least 50 scoreable golden sessions across realistic developer flows
• Mix of easy / medium / hard / overhead / untracked difficulty tiers
• Drop-in to existing build_dataset.py format (input prompt + expected_output JSON)
• Documented difficulty taxonomy and design rationale per session
• Both real-project (Meridian KAN-xxx) and generic-placeholder (PROJ-xxx) developer personas
Details

Reporter  Aditya Harish
Labels  evals, intelligence, dataset
Sprint  Sprint 12
Story points  5

ground_truth.reasoning: Brief 25s glance at the KAN-139 Jira ticket — user cmd-tabs to Chrome where this tab has been open since session 3, hovers/scrolls to re-check the acceptance criteria (50 scoreable sessions, mix of difficulty tiers, drop-in format, persona split), then cmd-tabs away. No action taken in Jira, no comment, no status change. Pure re-orientation between Claude Code interactions. Marked as overhead because the activity is reading-for-reference, not active task work. Sub-scoreable (timeline density only — won't enter the recent-sessions block).

sessions.design_notes: Pure context-only / sub-scoreable session — exists for narrative coherence between sessions 11 and 13, not for eval scoring. scoreable=false means build_synthetic_dataset.py's _scoreable filter will exclude this from both the Goldens output AND the recent-sessions context that subsequent scoreable sessions see. The realistic shape: developer takes a 25s break between two long Claude Code interactions to skim the ticket again. Although the KAN-139 page is on screen, no decision is made and no work happens — semantically NOT KAN-139 task work, just orientation. This is the difference between 'sessions that look at KAN-139 because they ARE KAN-139' (scoreable, sessions 5/8/11) and 'sessions that look at KAN-139 because they're re-orienting' (sub-scoreable, this one). Tests that the scoreable flag is the right granularity — a future eval architecture might choose to weight these differently rather than skip entirely.

  │         prep.headers['Idempotency-Key'] = self._idempotency_key\n    return prep │

⏺ Honest coverage map for sessions 1–6:

  **What this batch CAN detect:**

  1. *Keyword-mention false positive (session 3)* — if classifier picks PROJ-201
     or PROJ-215 from the Slack DM content, the exact-match metric flags it.
     Comparable to Dev A session 1 + 10.

  2. *Branch-name anchoring (sessions 1, 2, 5)* — if classifier misses PROJ-201
     when 'feat/proj-201-idempotency' is on screen, that's a baseline failure.

  3. *External research with recent context (session 4)* — if classifier labels
     the Stack Overflow page as 'untracked' or 'overhead' instead of PROJ-201,
     it's failing to use the recent-sessions block. This is the *only* session in
     the batch that meaningfully exercises the context-window architecture; if
     you A/B count_5 vs time_10m, this is one of the few where the choice could
     change the label.

  4. *Untracked-vs-decoy resistance (session 6)* — if classifier picks PROJ-230
     when the session is generic React work, that's the hallucinate-a-ticket
     failure mode. The most important catch in this batch.

  **What this batch CAN'T detect yet (sessions 7–35 must cover):**

  1. *Decoy clean-positive*. Session 6 proves the classifier shouldn't pick
     PROJ-230 when wrong, but doesn't prove it CAN pick PROJ-230 when right.
     Without a clean-positive PROJ-230 session, an over-cautious classifier that
     never picks PROJ-230 scores 100% on session 6 but fails silently on the
     real PROJ-230 case later. Session 7 or 8 needs to be the PROJ-230 positive
     (e.g., scaffolding UserLookupForm with /admin route + support vocabulary).

  2. *PROJ-210 → PROJ-225 confusion*. The whole 'analytics events look like email
     A/B' discrimination test from session 11's chat. No PROJ-210 work in this
     batch yet — no way to test the PROJ-225 decoy on the right adjacent ticket.

  3. *Ambiguous between two real tickets*. No session in this batch has two
     candidate tickets in genuine tension. Dev A's session 30 (KAN-139 vs KAN-105
     multi-task) is the comparable shape; Dev B needs at least one (e.g.,
     PROJ-204 webhook handler work that touches the same file as PROJ-201's
     idempotency change).

     decoy (synthetic adjacency). A stronger test is untracked + a *real* ticket
     that's tempting (e.g., the dev fixes a flaky test that isn't

python - parametrize a pytest fixture with deepeval Golden objects - Stack Overflow
Asked 7 months ago · Modified 4 months ago · 1,847 views
9
I'm running a deepeval suite where I load a Golden list from a JSON file and want each Golden to become its own pytest test case (so test failures surface per-Golden in the report, not as one bulk fail). The Goldens come from EvaluationDataset.from_json. My current fixture iterates them inline which collapses everything into one test.

  @pytest.fixture(scope='module')
  def goldens():
      return EvaluationDataset(goldens=json.loads(Path('.dataset.json').read_text())).goldens

  def test_classifier(goldens):
      for g in goldens:
          actual = classify_session(g.input)
          # ... metric.measure(g, actual) — all collapsed into one test ...

What's the cleanest parametrize pattern here? I want each Golden to be its own test_classifier[seed_id=N] in the pytest output.
  python   pytest   deepeval
asked Oct 14, 2025 at 14:22 by codeface3712 ✓
[09:20:54]
stackoverflow.com (Accepted answer)
Accepted answer · 14
Use pytest_generate_tests to expand the dataset at collection time, indirect=False so each Golden becomes a real test_case. Two pieces:

  # conftest.py
  from pathlib import Path
  import json
  from deepeval.dataset import EvaluationDataset, Golden

  def _load_goldens() -> list[Golden]:
      payload = json.loads(Path('.dataset.json').read_text())
      return [Golden(**g) for g in payload]

  def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc):
      if 'golden' in metafunc.fixturenames:
          goldens = _load_goldens()
          ids = [g.additional_metadata.get('seed_id', i) for i, g in enumerate(goldens)]
          metafunc.parametrize('golden', goldens, ids=[f'seed_id={i}' for i in ids])

  # test_classifier.py
  def test_classifier(golden):
      actual = classify_session_blocking(golden.input)
      case = LLMTestCase(input=golden.input, actual_output=actual,
                         expected_output=golden.expected_output)
      for metric in CLASSIFIER_METRICS:
          metric.measure(case)
          assert metric.is_successful(), f'{metric.__name__}: {metric.reason}'

Now pytest -v shows test_classifier[seed_id=1] PASSED / FAILED per Golden, and you can pytest -k 'seed_id=4' to debug a single case.

  Edited Oct 16, 2025 at 09:31 by codeface3712
  edited Mar 03, 2026 by anubhav-meta (added the additional_metadata.seed_id detail)
[09:21:08]
stackoverflow.com (scrolled — second answer)
2 answers — answer 2 · 3
If you want even finer control, use pytest_collection_modifyitems with a custom Item subclass. Heavier but lets you attach per-Golden metadata to the JUnit XML output for CI dashboards:

  class GoldenItem(pytest.Item):
      def __init__(self, name, parent, golden):
          super().__init__(name, parent)
          self.golden = golden
      def runtest(self):
          actual = classify_session_blocking(self.golden.input)
          # ... same metric loop ...
      def reportinfo(self):
          return self.fspath, 0, f'seed_id={self.golden.additional_metadata["seed_id"]}'

Usually overkill — pytest_generate_tests is enough.

pytest fixtures — pytest documentation
Fixtures are functions that have a return value that pytest sends to your test function. Use the fixture by adding it as a parameter of your test function.

  def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc):
      if 'fruit' in metafunc.fixturenames:

import os, sys
import json
from pathlib import Path
from deepeval.dataset import Golden

_SERVICES_DIR = Path(__file__).parent.parent.parent
sys.path.insert(0, str(_SERVICES_DIR))

os.environ.setdefault("DEEPEVAL_TELEMETRY_OPT_OUT", "YES")
os.environ.setdefault("OLLAMA_HOST", "http://localhost:11434")

_DATASET_PATH = Path(__file__).parent / ".dataset.json"


def _load_goldens() -> list[Golden]:
    """Load Goldens from .dataset.json — emits stable seed_id ordering."""
    if not _DATASET_PATH.exists():
        return []
    payload = json.loads(_DATASET_PATH.read_text())
    return [Golden(**g) for g in payload]


def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc):
    """Expand the Goldens list at collection time so each becomes its own test
    case. Test ids use additional_metadata.seed_id when present (matches the
    integer id from the seed file — easy to grep/cross-reference with the
    golden_seed/dev_*_sessions.json files).
    """
    if "golden" in metafunc.fixturenames:
        goldens = _load_goldens()
        ids = [
            f"seed_id={g.additional_metadata.get('seed_id', i)}"
            f"_diff={g.additional_metadata.get('difficulty', 'n/a')}"
            for i, g in enumerate(goldens)
        ]
        metafunc.parametrize("golden", goldens, ids=ids)

"""Parametrized eval entry point — one pytest test_case per Golden from
.dataset.json. Test ids are 'seed_id=N_diff=TIER' so failures surface per-Golden
in the report and can be filtered with: pytest -k 'diff=hard' or 'seed_id=15'.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import pytest
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase

from agents.run_task_linker_mlx import classify_session_blocking
from tests.evals.metrics import CLASSIFIER_METRICS


@pytest.mark.eval
def test_synthetic_classifier(golden):
    """Run the MLX classifier on a single Golden and assert exact-match metrics
    pass. Test id (set by pytest_generate_tests in conftest.py) encodes the seed
    id + difficulty tier — failure output shows immediately which case broke.
    """
    actual = classify_session_blocking(golden.input)
    case = LLMTestCase(
        input=golden.input,
        actual_output=actual,
        expected_output=golden.expected_output,
    )
    for metric in CLASSIFIER_METRICS:
        metric.measure(case)
        assert metric.is_successful(), (
            f"{metric.__name__} failed: {metric.reason}"
            f" · seed_id={golden.additional_metadata.get('seed_id')}"
            f" · difficulty={golden.additional_metadata.get('difficulty')}"
            f" · persona={golden.additional_metadata.get('persona')}"
        )

⏺ Two approaches — pick based on how often you'll filter:

  **Approach 1 (lightweight, recommended for now):** use `pytest -k` with the test-id
  substrings the conftest already emits:

    pytest services/tests/evals/test_synthetic_classifier.py -v -k 'diff=hard'
    pytest services/tests/evals/test_synthetic_classifier.py -v -k 'seed_id=15'
    pytest services/tests/evals/test_synthetic_classifier.py -v -k 'diff=hard or diff=hard-decoy'
    # filter persona via the additional_metadata in the id once we encode it:
    # → bump the conftest test_id format to also include persona

  Tradeoff: pytest -k is substring match, so 'diff=hard' also matches 'diff=hard-decoy'.
  Not always what you want.

  **Approach 2 (proper CLI flags via pytest plugin hook):** add custom --difficulty and
  --persona options to conftest.py. Skip-during-collection for non-matching cases —
  clean test runner output, exact-match semantics.

  Want me to apply Approach 2? Concretely it's another conftest.py change:

    def pytest_addoption(parser):
        parser.addoption('--difficulty', action='append', default=None,
                         help='Run only Goldens matching one of these difficulty tiers')
        parser.addoption('--persona', action='append', default=None,
                         help='Run only Goldens matching one of these personas')

    def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc):
        if 'golden' not in metafunc.fixturenames:
            return
        diff_filter    = metafunc.config.getoption('difficulty')
        persona_filter = metafunc.config.getoption('persona')
        goldens = _load_goldens()
        if diff_filter:
            goldens = [g for g in goldens if g.additional_metadata.get('difficulty') in diff_filter]
        if persona_filter:
            goldens = [g for g in goldens if g.additional_metadata.get('persona') in persona_filter]
        ids = [f"seed_id={g.additional_metadata.get('seed_id', i)}_diff={g.additional_metadata.get('difficulty', 'n/a')}_p={g.additional_metadata.get('persona', '?')[:6]}"
               for i, g in enumerate(goldens)]
        metafunc.parametrize('golden', goldens, ids=ids)

  Usage:
    pytest ... --difficulty hard --difficulty hard-decoy
    pytest ... --persona a_meridian
    pytest ... --difficulty medium --persona b_generic --difficulty hard

  Filters compose (AND across flags, OR within a flag). Empty filter = all Goldens
  (default behavior preserved).

  Worth adding now or wait until you actually need it?

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

REPO_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/../.." && pwd)"
DAEMON_PLIST="$HOME/Library/LaunchAgents/com.meridiona.mlx-server.plist"
LOG_DIR="$HOME/.meridian/logs"

mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR"
mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DAEMON_PLIST")"

echo "→ installing mlx-server launchd plist…"
launchctl unload "$DAEMON_PLIST" 2>/dev/null || true
cp "$REPO_DIR/services/scripts/com.meridiona.mlx-server.plist" "$DAEMON_PLIST"
launchctl load -w "$DAEMON_PLIST"
echo "✓ mlx-server daemon installed (persistent + on-demand merged, KAN-138)"

# PID file written by the daemon itself when it starts (RunAtLoad triggers it).
# On the merged plist the daemon may exit when idle — PID file may be stale
# on subsequent checks. So we only check on first install.
sleep 2
if [ -f "$HOME/.meridian/mlx_lm_server.pid" ]; then
  echo "✓ mlx-server up · pid=$(cat $HOME/.meridian/mlx_lm_server.pid)"
else
  echo "⚠ no PID file yet — daemon may be initializing, run launchctl list to verify"
fi

echo "→ tail logs: tail -f $LOG_DIR/mlx-server.log"

[Composing — 234 chars]
[09:28:24]
claude — meridian — TERMINAL
⏺ Three-step smoke test, ~30 seconds local:

  1. **Validate plist syntax** (catches XML/typing errors, doesn't touch launchd):
     plutil -lint services/scripts/com.meridiona.mlx-server.plist
     → 'OK' if XML is valid.

  2. **Dry-load against launchd** (catches RunAtLoad/KeepAlive inconsistencies):
     launchctl bootstrap gui/$(id -u) services/scripts/com.meridiona.mlx-server.plist
     launchctl print gui/$(id -u)/com.meridiona.mlx-server | grep -E 'state|last exit'
     → 'state = running' or 'state = waiting' is healthy. 'last exit' should be 0.
     Then unload to clean up:
     launchctl bootout gui/$(id -u)/com.meridiona.mlx-server

  3. **Full install script** (only if 1+2 pass):
     ./services/scripts/install-mlx-server-daemon.sh
     → tail logs/mlx-server.log to see the python interpreter actually start.

  RunAtLoad=true + KeepAlive=false is NOT a rejected combo on macOS launchd — it's
  documented as 'one-shot at boot then exit cleanly'. Common pattern for resource-
  conscious daemons. The only inconsistency launchd flags is RunAtLoad=false +
  KeepAlive=<dict that includes SuccessfulExit>, which we don't have.

  If you want belt-and-suspenders, add the plutil + bootstrap dry-run to install-
  mlx-server-daemon.sh as a guard. Want me to add it now?

Designing golden datasets for LLM classifiers — Eval Theatre
The cheapest way to ship a worse classifier is to evaluate it on a dataset that flatters it.

I spent the last year shipping classifier models at wendell.ai — three distinct production deployments, all session-classification flavored: customer support intent routing, sales-call topic extraction, and (most recently) a developer-activity classifier that maps screen captures to project tickets. Across all three, the single highest-leverage decision was the golden dataset, not the model architecture or the prompt.

Three findings, ordered by how surprised I was:

1. Synthetic data is fine — but only if you author for *failure modes*, not *coverage*.
2. The dataset author and the classifier should never be the same model family. Contamination is invisible at the metric level until you ablation.
3. 'Difficulty' is a worse stratification than 'failure mode targeted.' I'll explain.

Let's go in order.
[09:31:54]
Designing golden datasets for LLM classifiers — Eval Theatre (scrolled into section 1)
### 1. Authoring for failure modes, not coverage

The classical advice is 'pick examples that represent the full distribution.' If 60% of your production traffic is type A and 30% is type B and 10% is type C, your golden set should be 60/30/10. That's the coverage view.

It's wrong for classifiers. Here's why: the classifier you're evaluating is going to be RIGHT on the easy 70% of traffic regardless of model or prompt. What discriminates two model candidates is the hard 30% — the ambiguous, the keyword-baited, the same-app context-switched, the wrong-category-but-tempting cases. If your golden set is 60% easy, then the metric blurs all your discrimination power into the long tail.

Instead, author against *specific failure modes you've observed or suspect*:

- Keyword-mention overhead — where a ticket key appears in chat but the user isn't working on the ticket
- Same-app context switch — where the active tab changes but the surrounding chrome is identical  
- Short high-signal sessions — where a single command (e.g. `git checkout -b feat/XXX`) carries more signal than minutes of subsequent typing
- Decoy candidates — semantically-adjacent tickets that aren't the right answer
- Untracked-with-tempting-candidate — work that does NOT map to a candidate even though one is nearby

You should be able to point to every example in your golden set and say 'this catches failure mode N.' If you can't, that example is filler.
[09:32:06]
Designing golden datasets for LLM classifiers — Eval Theatre (scrolled — code excerpt)
[hover on a code block in section 1]
```python
# anti-pattern: distribute by token frequency
goldens = pd.read_json('traffic_sample.json').sample(500, weights='count')
# pattern: distribute by failure mode
goldens = sum([
    sample_keyword_mention_traps(40),
    sample_same_app_context_switch(40),
    sample_short_high_signal(30),
    sample_decoy_adjacent(30),
    sample_untracked_tempting(30),
    sample_easy_positive_baseline(60),   # baseline only; keep it small
    sample_overhead_clear(20),
], [])
```
The 'easy positive baseline' is the only place where coverage-thinking still applies — you need *some* easy positives to detect catastrophic regressions where the classifier breaks for trivially correct cases. But keep it under 25% of the set, or your discrimination power evaporates.
[09:32:18]
Designing golden datasets for LLM classifiers — Eval Theatre (section 2 begins)
### 2. Family contamination — invisible until ablation

When you generate synthetic goldens with an LLM, the natural choice is whichever LLM you have a relationship with. If you also evaluate an LLM from the same family, you have a contamination risk that doesn't show up in your metrics.

It's not that the evaluated model has 'seen' your goldens in training (it hasn't — the goldens didn't exist at training time). It's that the generating model and the evaluated model share *blind spots*. Things one finds easy, the other finds easy. Things one finds hard, the other finds hard. So your eval over-states accuracy on the cases that matter most: the hard ones.

Mitigation: cross-family sampling. If your production classifier is Llama, generate goldens with Claude or GPT. Better: human-author the hardest 20% of goldens, even if it costs a week. The 'hardest 20%' is where contamination bites worst, and where human judgment is most valuable.

Test for contamination by ablating: re-generate the dataset with a different family, re-run the eval, compare the deltas per difficulty tier. If the same-family eval shows materially higher accuracy on hard cases, you have a contamination signal.
[09:32:30]
Designing golden datasets for LLM classifiers — Eval Theatre (mid-article — section 3)
### 3. 'Difficulty' is the wrong stratification

Most golden datasets I've seen tag examples as easy / medium / hard. This sounds principled. It's not.

'Difficulty' is downstream of what the classifier finds difficult — which changes between models. A case that's hard for Llama is easy for Claude, or vice versa. So your difficulty tags are model-specific, not example-specific. If you swap classifiers, all your medium/hard tags are now noise, but you don't know which ones.

Better stratification: failure mode targeted. Each example is tagged with the specific class of mistake it tests for. 'keyword_mention_trap', 'same_app_context_switch', 'untracked_with_tempting_candidate'. Model-invariant. When you swap classifiers and accuracy on 'same_app_context_switch' goes from 92% to 67%, you know exactly what regressed.

This requires authors to commit to a taxonomy before generating, which slows initial dataset creation by ~30% in my experience. But it pays back the first time you actually swap models — you regain regression visibility immediately, instead of having to re-tag everything.
[09:32:48]
Designing golden datasets for LLM classifiers — Eval Theatre (scrolled — section 4)
### 4. Decoy candidates that can never be right

A subtle trap: you add 'decoy' candidates to your classifier prompt to test whether the model resists obvious-but-wrong matches. After 6 months of monitoring, you notice the decoys are NEVER the correct answer in your golden set. The model learns this. Decoy resistance metric stays at 100%, but in production where decoys ARE sometimes correct, the model under-picks them.

Symmetric decoy design is the fix: every decoy ticket needs at least one golden where IT is the correct answer. That feels paradoxical — 'a decoy that's correct isn't a decoy.' Right. The point is the decoy is decoy *for most adjacent sessions*, but a clean-positive for sessions that genuinely match it. Without the clean-positive examples, your model learns 'decoy candidate' as a category that always loses, which is exactly the wrong lesson.

In our pipeline we enforce: every decoy K appears in at least one golden as expected_output. If you can't construct a believable clean-positive for K, K isn't a real candidate, drop it.

[Sidebar: highlighted callout box]
"The ratio matters too. 1 clean-positive per ~5 decoy-resistance cases keeps both signals strong. More clean-positives degrade the decoy-resistance signal; fewer create the 'always-wrong' learning artifact."
[09:33:00]
Designing golden datasets for LLM classifiers — Eval Theatre (later scroll — section 5 + conclusion)
### 5. The untracked label is load-bearing

Every classifier I've shipped wanted to skip 'untracked' as an output. Customer-support: 'why would we have a customer message that isn't intent-classified?' Sales: 'every call must map to a deal stage.' Developer-activity: 'every session must map to a ticket.'

In each case, the team was wrong. ~15% of production traffic doesn't fit any candidate. If your classifier doesn't have an 'untracked' / 'none' output, those 15% get force-fit into the closest-but-wrong candidate, and you ship a model that's *confidently wrong* on a sixth of traffic.

The hard part: untracked is the easiest label to under-emit. Models hallucinate productive matches because that's what 'helpful' looks like. The eval needs to specifically grade 'classified as the wrong ticket when correct was untracked' as a regression-grade metric. Sloppy datasets don't include untracked cases, or include them only with no plausible candidate nearby — both blind your eval to the failure mode.

### Conclusion

If you take one thing from this: the golden set is the artifact you ship. The classifier is downstream of it. Spend the time.

Three actionable takeaways:
  • Author against failure modes, not traffic distribution
  • Avoid same-family contamination; ablation-test to catch what slipped through
  • Tag by failure mode, not difficulty; symmetric decoys; load-bearing untracked

Next post in this series (Friday): how we instrument the eval pipeline to fail loudly on regression, instead of silently averaging out.

Three failure modes this discriminates: (1) classifier picks KAN-139 because content overlap is ~80% — wrong, the user is consuming external knowledge, not producing the deliverable. The discriminator: no editor change, no Jira interaction, no commit, no file path under services/tests/evals/. (2) classifier picks overhead because session is in the distraction phase — wrong, this is genuinely work-relevant reading and the user is engaged (scrolling section-by-section, hovering code blocks, reading comments). Marking overhead loses the productivity signal. (3) classifier picks one of the decoy tickets (KAN-142 Worklog or KAN-145 Dashboard UI) — wrong, neither has any content overlap. The correct label 'untracked' is the third option a token-frequency classifier rarely emits. Tests whether the model has learned 'work + no candidate = untracked' as a first-class outcome. Without this case in the training/eval set, classifiers will collapse untracked into KAN-139 ~90% of the time. The article ITSELF talks about this exact failure mode in section 5 ('the untracked label is load-bearing') — a meta layer that makes the case especially good for showing classifier behavior on cases where the content explicitly mentions the correct label.

⏺ Three things to know about how the eval will treat session 29:

  1. **The classifier sees no recursion** — it sees a normal session_text that happens
     to describe a Claude Code conversation about authoring seed sessions. The fact that
     this session_text *is* one of those seed sessions is invisible to the model at
     inference time. It just sees Write(dev_a_sessions.json) + diff content + user
     approval. Normal KAN-139 work pattern.

  2. **The risk is at GENERATION time, not classification time.** I'm generating session

The follow-up Q&A about handling self-reference is an artifact-quality point that real engineering teams hit when synthetic datasets describe their own creation. [09:41:54]
claude — meridian — TERMINAL
⏺ Each pytest-xdist worker is a separate process — so by default each worker creates
  its own trace root. That's usually NOT what you want for eval runs (you can't compare
  traces across runs easily if each one has a different trace_id per worker).

  Two ways to fix it, ordered by effort:

  **Option A — set MERIDIAN_TRACEPARENT before invoking pytest:**
    export MERIDIAN_TRACEPARENT=00-$(uuidgen | tr -d '-' | head -c 32)-$(uuidgen | tr -d '-' | head -c 16)-01
    pytest -n 4 services/tests/evals/test_synthetic_classifier.py -v --persona a_meridian
  
  Now all 4 workers see the same traceparent in os.environ, and the render() span in
  each worker becomes a child of the shared root. The trace tree becomes:
  
    <shared-root-from-MERIDIAN_TRACEPARENT>
      ├── eval.render_dataset (worker 1 · 5 goldens)
      │     ├── eval.load_seed
      │     └── (events × 5)
      ├── eval.render_dataset (worker 2 · 5 goldens)
      ├── eval.render_dataset (worker 3 · 5 goldens)
      └── eval.render_dataset (worker 4 · 5 goldens)

  **Option B — add a pytest plugin hook that generates and exports a root span:**
    # services/tests/evals/conftest.py
    @pytest.fixture(scope='session', autouse=True)
    def _eval_run_span():
        with _tracer.start_as_current_span('eval.run') as root:
            os.environ['MERIDIAN_TRACEPARENT'] = _format_traceparent(root.get_span_context())
            yield root
  
  pytest-xdist's master process runs the session fixture once, sets the env var,
  workers pick it up. Cleaner because the trace root has metadata (test marker,
  config flags) that a manually-generated UUID doesn't.

  My recommendation: **Option B**, but feature-gate it behind OTEL_SDK_DISABLED so
  CI runs without OpenObserve don't pay the no-op overhead. Want me to apply Option B
  to conftest.py now, or queue for after we land the OTLP instrumentation on its own?

# Meridian — Claude Code Instructions

Meridian is a single-process Rust daemon that reads screenpipe's SQLite database and normalises raw screen-capture frames into structured, app-based activity sessions stored in its own SQLite database at `~/.meridian/meridian.db`. A Next.js dashboard and a TypeScript MCP server sit alongside the daemon.

---

## Hard Rules

- Do what has been asked; nothing more, nothing less
- NEVER create files unless absolutely necessary — prefer editing existing files
- NEVER create documentation files unless explicitly requested
- ALWAYS read a file before editing it
- NEVER commit secrets, credentials, or `.env` files
- Keep files under 500 lines; split when a file grows beyond that

## Python agent service (`services/`)

A Python service runs alongside the Rust daemon and writes Jira task mappings + multi-label dimension tags into `meridian.db`. The classification engine uses hermes `AIAgent` to match sessions to tasks.

For installation, ops (launchd daemon, hot-toggle, single-session inspector), see `services/README.md`. For the deep technical reference (classification logic, scoring formulas, recipes for tuning prompts / debugging misclassifications), see `services/agents/README.md`.

### Hard rules

- **Every `.py` file in `services/agents/` must start with a `\"\"\"…\"\"\"` module docstring** describing its purpose. The Rust/TS file-header convention does not apply — Python uses docstrings.
- **Don't break the cursor monotonicity invariant in `tagger.run_once`.** `agent_cursor.last_session_id` only advances.
- **`ticket_links` and `session_dimensions` writes must be idempotent.** Both tables have UNIQUE / composite-PK constraints with explicit `ON CONFLICT … DO UPDATE` policies.

### Quick command refrence

  1 | // meridian — normalises screenpipe activity into structured app sessions
  2 | use anyhow::Context as _;

  7 |     propagation::TraceContextPropagator,

 10 | };
 11 | use tracing_opentelemetry::OpenTelemetryLayer;
 12 | use tracing_subscriber::{layer::SubscriberExt, util::SubscriberInitExt, EnvFilter};
 13 |
 14 | pub fn setup(service_name: &'static str) -> anyhow::Result<()> {
 15 |     let otlp_endpoint = std::env::var("MERIDIAN_OTLP_ENDPOINT").ok();
 16 |     let oo_auth = std::env::var("MERIDIAN_OO_AUTH").ok();
 17 |
 18 |     if otlp_endpoint.is_none() {
 19 |         // No endpoint configured — install a no-op subscriber only.
 20 |         tracing_subscriber::registry()
 21 |             .with(EnvFilter::from_default_env())
 22 |             .with(tracing_subscriber::fmt::layer())
 23 |             .init();
 24 |         return Ok(());
 25 |     }
 26 |
 27 |     let endpoint = otlp_endpoint.unwrap();
 28 |     let mut headers = std::collections::HashMap::new();
 29 |     if let Some(auth) = oo_auth {
 30 |         headers.insert("Authorization".to_string(), format!("Basic {auth}"));
 31 |     }
 32 |
 33 |     let tracer = opentelemetry_otlp::new_pipeline()
 34 |         .tracing()
 35 |         .with_exporter(
 36 |             opentelemetry_otlp::new_exporter()
 37 |                 .http()
 38 |                 .with_endpoint(&endpoint)
 39 |                 .with_headers(headers),
 40 |         )
 41 |         .with_trace_config(
 42 |             sdktrace::config()
 43 |                 .with_sampler(Sampler::AlwaysOn)
 44 |                 .with_resource(opentelemetry_sdk::Resource::new(vec![
 45 |                     opentelemetry::KeyValue::new("service.name", service_name),
 46 |                 ])),
 47 |         )
 48 |         .install_batch(runtime::Tokio)
 49 |         .context("failed to install OTLP tracer")?;
 50 |
 51 |     global::set_text_map_propagator(TraceContextPropagator::new());
 52 |     global::set_tracer_provider(tracer.provider().unwrap());
 53 |
 54 |     tracing_subscriber::registry()
 55 |         .with(EnvFilter::from_default_env())
 55 |         .with(tracing_subscriber::fmt::layer())
 56 |         .with(OpenTelemetryLayer::new(tracer))
 57 |         .init();
 58 |
 59 |     Ok(())
 60 | }
 61 |

 64 | }

window_titles.window_name: Your AI Evaluations Are Probably Wrong — Hamel Husain — Google Chrome
Evaluating LLMs Is a Minefield — Google Chrome

sessions.session_text:
[09:56:05]
Google Chrome
Your AI Evaluations Are Probably Wrong
Hamel Husain · hamel.ai · May 2025

Contents
1. The evaluation fallacy
2. Why golden datasets mislead
3. The calibration problem
4. What actually works
5. Practical recommendations

─────────────────────────────────────

The evaluation fallacy

Every ML team I talk to has the same story: they built an eval suite, the
numbers looked good, they shipped, and the product got worse.

The problem is structural. When you use accuracy on a held-out test set as
your north star, you're measuring how well your model fits the distribution
of your test set — not how well it serves users. These two things look
identical on a spreadsheet and diverge catastrophically in production.

Golden datasets: the calibration problem

A golden dataset is only as good as the labels. And labelling is hard:

  • Inter-annotator agreement on ambiguous cases is typically 60–70%,
    meaning your ground truth has 30–40% noise by construction.
  • Human raters anchor to surface features (keywords, app names) rather
    than intent — the same failure mode you're trying to catch in the model.
  • Golden datasets go stale. A label that was correct six months ago may
    be wrong today as product semantics shift.

The deeper problem: most teams validate their eval by checking whether it
correlates with their intuition. But intuition is what you're trying to
replace with measurement. Circular validation produces circular confidence.

[09:57:30]
Your AI Evaluations Are Probably Wrong — Hamel Husain

What actually works

The teams that get eval right share three practices:

1. Slice-based evaluation over aggregate metrics
   Don't report a single accuracy number. Report accuracy per input
   category, per difficulty tier, per user segment. A 90% headline number
   can hide a 40% failure rate on the cases that matter most.

2. Failure-mode-driven dataset construction
   Don't sample randomly from your production distribution. Deliberately
   construct cases that target known failure modes: keyword false positives,
   context contamination, adversarial inputs. If your dataset doesn't
   include the failure modes you're worried about, it won't detect regressions
   in those modes.

3. Live traffic shadow evaluation
   Run your new model on a sample of live traffic in shadow mode before
   promoting it. Human-review the cases where old and new disagree — that
   disagreement set is where your model is changing behaviour.

[09:58:20]
Your AI Evaluations Are Probably Wrong — Hamel Husain

Practical recommendations

  ✓ Keep your golden dataset small and high-quality: 50–200 cases is enough
    if every case targets a documented failure mode. 10,000 noisy cases is
    worse than 100 carefully constructed ones.
  ✓ Include 'sanity check' easy cases — not to pad your score but to act
    as tripwires that catch catastrophic regressions immediately.
  ✓ Version your prompts alongside your eval results. A score without the
    prompt version it was measured against is meaningless.
  ✓ When your model improves on the golden set but degrades in production,
    your golden set is wrong — not your model.

Evaluating LLMs Is a Minefield — Google Chrome
(new tab — follow-up article linked from comments)

Evaluating LLMs Is a Minefield
Eugene Yan · eugeneyan.com · April 2025

The three failure modes of LLM evaluation:

1. Evaluator-evaluated alignment
   If you use the same model family to evaluate as you're testing, you
   get correlated errors. GPT-4 judging GPT-4 outputs systematically
   over-scores certain patterns that GPT-4 itself exhibits.

2. Metric gaming
   Models fine-tuned to score well on a benchmark often do so by learning
   the benchmark's idiosyncrasies, not by improving on the underlying task.
   This is Goodhart's law applied to evals.

3. Distribution mismatch
   The eval distribution and the production distribution diverge over time.
   Live traffic shifts; golden datasets don't unless you actively maintain them.

The article recommends a rotation policy: retire 20% of your golden dataset
every quarter, replacing it with cases sampled from recent production failures.

SELECT
    a.id,
    a.app_name,
    a.started_at,
    a.duration_s,
    LENGTH(a.session_text)                              AS text_len,
    ROUND(LENGTH(a.session_text)
          / CAST(a.duration_s AS REAL), 1)              AS chars_per_sec,
    tl.task_key,
    ROUND(tl.confidence, 2)                             AS conf,
    json_extract(a.window_titles, '$[0].window_name')   AS top_window
FROM app_sessions a
JOIN ticket_links tl ON tl.session_id = a.id
WHERE a.duration_s  > 120
  AND LENGTH(a.session_text) > 4000
  AND tl.confidence  > 0.78
  AND tl.routing     = 'auto'
ORDER BY tl.confidence DESC, a.duration_s DESC
LIMIT 25;

sessions.design_notes: HARD case -- OTel traces in OpenObserve create maximum KAN-136 temptation. The correct identification requires understanding: service=meridian-eval (the eval service, not meridian-etl), the spans being read are eval.classify results (classifier outputs), accuracy.task_key / accuracy.both are eval metrics. audio_snippets.text: Aditya: the GlobalTracerProvider wrapper type only exposes shutdown, not force_flush. If you want force_flush you need to hold the SDK type directly. OnceCell is the cleanest way -- set it once in setup() and read it in shutdown().

Developer: got it. OnceCell of the SDK provider, call force_flush before the global shutdown. That matches what the Python side does. I'll do it when I pick up KAN-136 properly -- after the golden dataset is done.

Aditya: also the traceparent propagation -- pass it as an HTTP header on the classify request. The Python server reads it with TraceContextPropagator.extract. That gives you the full end-to-end trace from poll_tick down to classify_sessions.

Aditya: I'll review the PR seeds tomorrow. The design_notes field is really useful -- makes it easy to see what failure mode each session is targeting without having to reverse-engineer the session_text.
